content.heading: Data Types — what kind of thing is this?

blocks.text: Every value in JavaScript has a type. The type determines what you can do with it. You can't do math with text, and you can't use string methods on a number.

blocks.label: Remember!
blocks.text: Knowing the types helps you avoid bugs that seem totally random but have a very simple cause.
You can check the type of a value using the `typeof` operator.

blocks.desc: The typeof operator tells you what the type is for 'variableName'. It returns a string of the name of the type: 'number', 'string', or 'boolean'.

blocks.label: The most common beginner trap
blocks.text: If a number comes from user input or an HTML field, it arrives as a STRING. So '5' + 5 gives you '55'. Always check your types when math isn't working the way you expect.

exercises.title: What type is this?
tasks: What is the data type of this value: '100'?
exercises.hint: Look closely. Does this value have quotes around it?
exercises.explanation: '100' has quotes, which makes it a string — not a number. typeof '100' returns 'string'. This is the same trap that breaks game math when scores come from text fields.

exercises.title: What does typeof return?
exercises.hint: typeof always returns a string — the name of the type, in lowercase.
exercises.explanation: typeof always returns a string — the name of the type. The boolean value true has type 'boolean'. It won't return true or 1.

exercises.title: Sort by type
tasks: Drag each value into the correct type bucket.
exercises.hint: Quotes always make a string. true and false without quotes are booleans. A declared but unassigned variable is undefined.
exercises.explanation: 'false' in quotes is a string — it just looks like a boolean. '42' in quotes is a string — it just looks like a number. The quotes are always the deciding factor.

exercises.title: Drop the typeof keyword
tasks: Drop the correct keyword to check the type of each variable.
exercises.hint: The operator that checks types is two words joined together. Check the spelling carefully.
exercises.explanation: The operator is typeof — one word, all lowercase. 'type', 'typeOf', and 'getType' don't exist in JavaScript and will throw an error.

exercises.title: Pick the right typed value
tasks: Drop the correct value for each variable so it has the right type: score = number, playerName = string, isGameOver = boolean.
exercises.hint: Numbers have no quotes. Strings have quotes. Booleans are just true or false, no quotes.
exercises.explanation: '100' looks like a number but is a string. 'false' looks like a boolean but is a string. Alex without quotes would be an undefined variable reference. The correct picks are 100 (number), 'Alex' (string), and false (boolean).

exercises.title: Log the types in order
tasks: Put these lines in the correct order so each variable is declared before its typeof is logged.
exercises.hint: You must declare a variable before you can use typeof on it.
exercises.explanation: Each variable must be declared with let before typeof can be used on it. Declarations always come first, then the typeof logs.

exercises.title: Type the typeof expressions
tasks: Type the variable name inside each typeof call so it logs the type of each game variable.
exercises.hint: typeof takes the variable name right after it — no parentheses needed. typeof score, typeof playerName.
exercises.explanation: typeof score logs 'number', typeof playerName logs 'string', typeof isGameOver logs 'boolean'. These are the three types you'll use constantly in Simon Says.

exercises.title: Does 'true' equal true?
exercises.hint: === checks both the value AND the type. Are these two things the same type?
exercises.explanation: === checks both value and type. 'true' is a string and true is a boolean — different types, so === returns false. This is exactly why isGameOver = 'true' would silently break a game-over check. Remove the quotes to get the actual boolean.

exercises.title: Fix the types
tasks: Both variables have the wrong type. Fix them by typing the correct values — isGameOver should be a boolean, score should be a number.
exercises.hint: Remove the quotes to turn a string into its real type. false with no quotes is a boolean. 250 with no quotes is a number.
exercises.explanation: Reassigning without quotes converts the variables from strings to their correct types. false (no quotes) is a boolean. 250 (no quotes) is a number. typeof would now return 'boolean' and 'number' instead of 'string'.

exercises.title: Find the type bug
tasks: Hit Run — see what typeof prints for each variable.
One variable has the wrong type. Find it using the typeof output.
Fix that variable so it has the correct type. Hit Run to confirm.
exercises.hint: score should be a number. If typeof says 'string', remove the quotes from its value on line 2.

steps.text: Hit Run — read the typeof output for each variable. Which one looks wrong?
score shows 'string' but it should be a number. Fix line 2: remove the quotes from 120. Hit Run.
typeof now shows 'number' for score. Quotes were the only difference — and they change everything.

exercises.title: Fix the broken addition
tasks: Hit Run and read the output. The total shows '5010' instead of 60.
The typeof output tells you why — playerInput is a string, not a number.
Fix it: wrap playerInput in Number() when building total. Hit Run to confirm total is 60.
exercises.hint: Number('50') converts the string '50' to the number 50. Use it when you know a value should be a number.

steps.text: Hit Run. See '5010' printed — playerInput is a string, so + joined instead of added.
Fix total: change line 4 to: let total = Number(playerInput) + bonus; Hit Run.
Number() is your escape hatch whenever a value arrives as a string but needs to be treated as a number.

exercises.title: Build the type report
tasks: Use a template literal to log each variable's value and its type.
Do this for all four Simon Says variables. Hit Run when all four are logged.
exercises.hint: Combine template literals with typeof: `score: ${score} (type: ${typeof score})`

steps.text: Log playerName with its type. Write: console.log(`playerName: ${playerName} (type: ${typeof playerName})`); Hit Run.
Add the same for score and level. Hit Run.
Add isGameOver. Hit Run to confirm all four variables are logged.
You can now inspect any variable's type at a glance — a key debugging skill for Simon Says and beyond.

challenges.title: The scoreboard display
challenges.description: A game needs to display a player's name and score. Right now it's just printing two separate values. Fix the code so it prints exactly: 'Jordan - 340 pts', first using + concatenation, then using a template literal.
challenges.hint: The output should be 'Jordan - 340 pts'. You need the dash, the spaces, and the word 'pts'. None of those are in the variables.
challenges.explanation: Variables only contain what you put in them. The dash, spaces, and 'pts' are not stored anywhere. You have to add them yourself. With +: playerName + ' - ' + score + ' pts'. With a template literal: `${playerName} - ${score} pts`.

challenges.title: The broken name badge machine
challenges.description: A school event needs name badges. Each badge should say: 'Hi, my name is Jordan Lee and I am from Atlanta.' But the machine is printing 'Hi, my name is JordanLeeAtlanta', with no spaces or punctuation. Find what's wrong and fix it.
challenges.hint: When you join strings with +, JavaScript adds no spaces for you. Every space has to be written explicitly.

checks.includes: Hi, my name is Jordan Lee and I am from Atlanta.
checks.label: prints the correct badge text

challenges.explanation: firstName + lastName joins them with nothing in between, giving 'JordanLee'. You need a space between them: firstName + ' ' + lastName. Always check every join point for missing spaces.

challenges.title: The game state inspector
challenges.description: A Simon Says game has four variables. The game never ends even when isGameOver should be true. When you log isGameOver it prints true, but the game keeps going. Use typeof to inspect all four variables and find the one that isn't the type you'd expect. Then fix it.
challenges.hint: === checks both the value AND the type. 'true' and true look the same when logged, but typeof will reveal the difference.
challenges.explanation: isGameOver is set to 'true' in quotes, making it a string, not a boolean. The condition isGameOver === true fails because 'true' (string) is not the same as true (boolean). typeof isGameOver returns 'string', which reveals the bug. Fix: remove the quotes so isGameOver = true.
